expectations.expectation: Investigation includes environment/dependency analysis in addition to error and change analysis
expectations.justification: The investigation includes a timeline table analyzing both environment changes (CI runner downgrade from 4-core to 2-core, 2 weeks ago) and dependency changes (Node 18.17 to 18.19, 1 week ago). It also examines resource contention effects: 'On 2-core machines, they're contending for CPU, making fulfillment consistently slower.' This covers error analysis, change analysis, and environment/dependency analysis.

expectations.expectation: Investigation identifies the fixed sleep(2000) as a timing assumption that doesn't account for variable fulfillment time (500ms-3000ms)
expectations.justification: Under 'The critical timing issue': 'The test waits exactly 2000ms for all fulfillment jobs to complete. But the fulfillment worker takes between 500ms and 3000ms per job. If any of the 3 jobs takes longer than 2 seconds, the test fails.'

expectations.expectation: Investigation correlates the failure onset with the CI runner downgrade from 4-core to 2-core machines 2 weeks ago
expectations.justification: The timeline table explicitly shows the correlation: 'CI runner downgraded from 4-core to 2-core | 2 weeks ago | Less CPU = slower job processing' alongside 'Test started failing intermittently | 2 weeks ago | Exact match'. The Node version bump is correctly ruled out as occurring after the failures started.

expectations.expectation: Investigation cross-references the timing: fulfillment takes up to 3000ms but test only waits 2000ms, and slower CI runners exacerbate the gap
expectations.justification: The investigation connects both factors: the timing section identifies the 2000ms vs 3000ms mismatch, and the environment section explains why slower hardware worsens it: 'On 4-core machines, the fulfillment worker, job queue, database, and test runner all had dedicated cores. On 2-core machines, they're contending for CPU, making fulfillment consistently slower and pushing more jobs past the 2-second window.'

expectations.expectation: Hypothesis identifies the fragile sleep-based waiting pattern as the root cause, not a code bug in the order processing logic
expectations.justification: Root Cause section: 'This is not an application code bug. The order processing logic is correct. This is a test infrastructure issue: the test uses a fixed sleep(2000) to wait for asynchronous job completion, but the actual fulfillment time can be up to 3000ms (or more under resource contention). The sleep(2000) is a fragile timing assumption that breaks under slower environments.'

expectations.expectation: Hypothesis is verified by explaining the ~30% failure rate (correlates with fulfillment time distribution exceeding 2s more often on slower hardware)
expectations.justification: The investigation states: 'The ~30% failure rate makes sense: if fulfillment time is roughly uniformly distributed between 500ms-3000ms, on a 2-core machine with overhead, there's a meaningful probability that at least one of the 3 concurrent jobs exceeds 2 seconds total (including queue overhead).' This connects the failure rate to the fulfillment time distribution on slower hardware.

expectations.expectation: Fix replaces the fixed sleep with condition-based polling or retry-until-condition (e.g., poll order status with timeout)
expectations.justification: The fix provides a waitForCondition helper that polls every 500ms with a 15-second timeout, replacing the fixed sleep(2000). The test uses it to check whether all order statuses equal 'fulfilled'. The helper is designed for reuse across other tests.

expectations.expectation: A test or demonstration is provided showing why the sleep-based approach is fragile before implementing the fix
expectations.justification: No test or code demonstration is provided before the fix. The transcript explains the fragility in prose (timing analysis, 30% failure rate explanation, 4-core vs 2-core comparison) but does not write a failing test, create a mock-based reproduction, or provide any concrete code artifact demonstrating the sleep-based approach's failure before implementing the polling fix.

expectations.expectation: Quality review is performed on the fix to verify it doesn't introduce flaky behavior of its own
expectations.justification: The 'Key properties of this fix' section explicitly addresses flakiness concerns: (1) 'No fixed sleep' eliminates the timing assumption, (2) 'Generous timeout' of 15 seconds covers slow environments, (3) 'Polling interval' of 500ms balances responsiveness with DB load, (4) 'Clear failure mode' ensures timeout produces a diagnostic message. This constitutes a quality assessment examining whether the fix introduces its own flaky behavior.

expectations.expectation: Agent identifies this as a test infrastructure issue, not an application code bug, and communicates this distinction clearly
expectations.justification: Root Cause: 'This is not an application code bug. The order processing logic is correct. This is a test infrastructure issue.' Additional Recommendations reinforces: 'Don't revert the CI runner change: The 2-core machines exposed a pre-existing fragility. Going back to 4-core would mask the problem.' The distinction is communicated clearly throughout.

eval_feedback: Strong performance overall. The single FAIL (no test/demonstration before fix) reflects a difference in methodology -- the without_skill transcript explains the fragility analytically but does not produce a concrete code artifact demonstrating it.

The recommendation 'Don't revert the CI runner change' is a valuable insight not captured by any expectation -- it shows the agent understands that masking the symptom is worse than fixing the root cause.

The quality review via 'Key properties' is adequate but less rigorous than a dedicated adversarial review examining edge cases. It addresses the main concern (flakiness) but does not explore scenarios like stuck orders or polling overhead under load.
